28: ke-mareh haqeshet asher yihyeh beanav beyom hagashem ken mareh hanog saviv hu mareh demut kevod YHWH: 'Like the appearance of the bow that is in the cloud on the day of rain, so was the appearance of the brightness all around. Such was the appearance of the likeness of the glory of YHWH.' Ezekiel's theophany of the divine chariot-throne (merkabah) is the foundation of Jewish mystical speculation. His careful qualification of language — 'likeness of the glory of YHWH' rather than 'glory of YHWH' — maintains divine transcendence even in the vision. John of Revelation reuses Ezekiel's visionary vocabulary (the four living creatures of Ezek 1 reappear in Revelation 4:6-8; the rainbow around the throne in Revelation 4:3 echoes Ezekiel 1:28), grounding the Christological throne-vision in the Ezekielian framework.
1: The opening date formula places Ezekiel in 593 BCE (the thirtieth year, probably Ezekiel's own age — the year a Levite was to begin priestly service, Numbers 4:3). The setting is the Chebar Canal in Babylonia, among the first exiles deported in 597 BCE. The phrase marot elohim (marʾôt ʾĕlōhîm) — 'visions of God' — signals that what follows is not ordinary prophetic speech but direct visual encounter with the divine; the plural marʾôt (visions) is the standard term for visionary experience in later Hebrew prophecy (cf. Daniel 8:1; Zechariah 1:8).
2: galut (gālût): 'captivity, exile.' The technical term for the exilic community — those carried away from the land. Ezekiel's call comes in the midst of the exile, not in the land; the divine glory is not confined to Zion but can be encountered at the Chebar River in Babylon. This is theologically radical: YHWH is present with the exiles, not merely with those who remain in the land. The Joiachin deportation (597 BCE) is the reference point — the fifth year of King Jehoiachin's exile.
3: yad YHWH (yad Yhwh): 'the hand of YHWH.' The formula for prophetic compulsion — the overpowering divine energy that takes hold of the prophet (cf. 1 Kings 18:46; Isaiah 8:11; Jeremiah 15:17). In Ezekiel it appears with particular frequency (Ezekiel 1:3; Ezekiel 3:14, 22; Ezekiel 8:1; Ezekiel 37:1; Ezekiel 40:1), marking each major visionary episode. The hand of YHWH functions as both a physical sensation and a metaphor for the divine initiative: Ezekiel does not choose to enter the vision — it seizes him. hayah (hāyâ) 'was/came upon' — the perfect tense in Hebrew here marks a completed decisive event.
4: ruach searah (rûaḥ sĕʿārâh): 'stormy wind/whirlwind.' The theophany begins with cosmic storm — the same elemental context as Sinai (Exodus 19:16-18) and the Job theophany (Job 38:1). anan gadol (ʿānān gādōl): 'great cloud' — divine cloud always signals the Shekinah presence (Exodus 16:10; 1 Kings 8:10-11). esh mitlaqaqhat (ʾēš mitlaqqaḥat): 'flashing/flickering fire' — the continuous participle suggests ongoing motion, not a single flash. hashmal (ḥašmal): one of the most discussed words in biblical Hebrew; the LXX renders it ēlektron (electrum, a gold-silver alloy) — a gleaming, luminous metal. The Mishnah restricts public teaching of this passage (m. Hagigah Ezekiel 2:1), reflecting the mystery surrounding Ezekiel's throne-vision.
5: arba hayyot (ʾarbaʿ ḥayyôt): 'four living creatures.' Hayyot (plural of ḥayyâ, living creature) becomes in Ezekiel the term for the beings supporting the divine chariot-throne; they are identified as cherubim in chapter 10. In John's Apocalypse (Revelation 4:6-8) the same four creatures appear surrounding the heavenly throne, picking up Ezekiel's vision directly. The number four represents the four directions — the divine throne encompasses the totality of spatial reality.
6: Each creature has four wings — two spread upward meeting above, two covering the body. The four-wing configuration differs from the seraphim of Isaiah 6 (six wings) but shares the function of covering and flight. The wings touching one another above forms the kappōret-like space of the divine presence, recalling the cherubim whose wings overshadowed the ark (Exodus 25:20). Ezekiel's beings are thus mobile cherubim — the portable ark-throne of the God who travels to Babylon with his people.
7: The feet are described as regel yeshara (regel yĕšārâ): 'straight/single foot' — possibly hooves rather than articulated feet, gleaming like polished bronze (nechoshet qalal, nĕḥōšet qālāl). Bronze/copper is consistently the metal of divine judgment in Ezekiel and Daniel (Daniel 10:6; Revelation 1:15). The sparkling bronze feet that move without turning sideways represent the directional omnipresence of the divine will.
8: Human hands under their wings — yedot adam (yĕdôt ʾādām) — the creatures have both animal/celestial features and human hands, combining heavenly and earthly registers. The four-sided symmetry (hands on all four sides, faces on all four sides) means the divine chariot has no front or back — it operates equally in all directions simultaneously. This is a theological statement: divine action has no preferred direction, no blind spot.
9: Their wings touch one another — the connected wings create a unified covering over the expanse between them. When they move they go straight forward without turning (lo yisabu belekhtam, lōʾ yissabbû bĕlektām) — the Hebrew emphasizes directional constancy. The chariot-throne moves in any direction while the creatures themselves maintain their orientation; it is the wheels, not the creatures, that change direction. This engineering-like precision in the description reflects Ezekiel's priestly attention to exact sacred-space geometry.
10: The four faces — pene adam (pĕnê ʾādām): human face (front right); pene aryeh (pĕnê ʾaryēh): lion face (right); pene shor (pĕnê šôr): ox face (left); pene nesher (pĕnê nešer): eagle face (back). The four faces represent the four domains of living creatures (human, wild animal, domestic animal, bird) — the divine throne encompasses all creaturely modes of existence. Irenaeus (2nd century) identified these four faces with the four Gospels (human=Matthew, lion=John, ox=Luke, eagle=Mark — or in various orderings), a tradition that shaped medieval Christian iconography of the four evangelists.
11: Two wings spread upward and touched another's wings above; two wings covered the body. The spreading wings above form the firmament space (the expanse, rāqîaʿ, mentioned in Ezekiel 1:22-26) while the covering wings preserve modesty — the covering of the divine presence is always paired with disclosure (cf. the seraphim covering faces and feet in Isaiah 6:2). The creatures' posture enacts both revelation and hiddenness simultaneously.
12: Each creature goes straight forward — wherever the spirit goes, they go. ruach (rûaḥ) here means either 'spirit' (animating will) or 'wind' (direction) — the ambiguity is probably intentional. The creatures' motion is Spirit-directed: they do not move by their own initiative but by the ruach's leading. This pictures the total responsiveness of the divine chariot-mechanism to the divine will — no friction, no lag, no resistance.
13: Their appearance was like burning coals of fire (like the appearance of torches, legaḥălê ʾēš), flashing up and down among the creatures. Fire moving among the living beings connects to the divine fire that accompanies theophany throughout the OT (burning bush, pillar of fire, Sinai). The fire that 'moves back and forth' (mithallek) is the same participle used for God 'walking' in the garden (Genesis 3:8) — the divine presence in perpetual active motion.
14: The creatures dart (ratsot, rāṣôʾ) and return (shov, šôb) like the appearance of a flash of lightning (bazaq, bāzāq). The darting-and-returning motion of lightning is the closest natural analogy for the otherworldly speed of the divine chariot-creatures. The darting and returning movement also evokes the rabbinic image of the ma'ase merkabah (chariot work) — the mystical contemplation of this passage that became foundational for Jewish mysticism.
15: As I looked at the living creatures, I saw a wheel on the earth beside each living creature — ophan (ʾôp̄ān): wheel. The four wheels beside the four creatures introduce the chariot's mobile infrastructure. arba panav: the four of them — each wheel has four faces (is equidirectional). The wheels are the interface between the heavenly chariot and the earthly surface, enabling movement in any direction.
16: The wheels' appearance: ke-ein tarshish (kĕʿên taršîš): 'like chrysolite/beryl' — a yellow-green gemstone (Tarshish stone; Daniel 10:6 uses the same). The four wheels all have the same form. ophan betoch ophan (ʾôp̄ān bĕtôk ʾôp̄ān): 'wheel within a wheel' — the most discussed image of the vision. The concentric-wheel structure probably represents a gyroscope-like mechanism enabling movement in any direction without turning. This image became iconic in Jewish mysticism and has been widely noted as an analogy for complex mechanical systems.
17: When they moved they went in any of the four directions without turning (lo yisabu belekhtam): the directional freedom of the wheel-within-wheel system. Four-directional omnidirectional movement without pivoting is mechanically impossible for ordinary wheels — the vision describes a supernatural mobility system. The theological point: God's governance of history moves in any direction simultaneously without the limitations of fixed orientation.
18: gavoah (gābōah): 'height/loftiness.' The rims of the four wheels were tall and awesome (yira, yîrāʾ, inspiring fear). The rims were full of eyes (einayim, ʿênayim) all around the four of them. Eyes on the rims: the divine chariot's wheels are all-seeing; every movement is watched in all directions simultaneously. In Revelation 4:6 the four living creatures are 'full of eyes all around and within,' combining the eyes of the chariot creatures and the eyes of Ezekiel's wheels.
19: When the living creatures moved, the wheels moved beside them; when the creatures rose from the earth, the wheels also rose. The total synchrony between living creatures and wheels: the organic beings and the mechanical system move as one unified entity. This synchrony will become the basis for the visionary language of divine-human integration in Ezekiel — the prophet's movements are eventually synchronized with divine instruction in the same way.
20: Wherever the ruach wished to go, they went — wherever the ruach went, the wheels went with them, for the ruach of the living creatures was in the wheels. ruach hahayyah beopanim (rûaḥ haḥayyâ bāʾôp̄annîm): 'the ruach of the living creature in the wheels.' The animating ruach pervades both the organic creatures and the mechanical wheels — there is no division between the spiritual and the material in the divine chariot. This anticipates Ezekiel's pneumatology: the ruach that animates the chariot is the same ruach that will animate the valley of dry bones (Ezekiel 37:9-14).
21: When they rose, the wheels rose; when they stopped, the wheels stopped; when they lifted themselves from the earth, the wheels lifted alongside them. The triple correspondence seals the synchrony described in Ezekiel 1:19-20. The repetition of the pattern is characteristic of Ezekiel's priestly precision — like the tabernacle instructions of Exodus that repeat specifications in both command and execution forms, Ezekiel repeats the chariot's movement principles to establish their reliability as observable law.
22: raqia (rāqîaʿ): 'expanse/firmament.' The same word used for the sky-dome of Genesis 1:6-8. qerach hanora (qeraḥ hannôrāʾ): 'awesome crystal/ice' — qeraḥ means frost or ice; the radiant crystal platform stretched over the heads of the living creatures like the icy brightness of the sky. The expanse is simultaneously the floor of the divine throne-platform and the ceiling of the creaturely domain — marking the boundary between the earthly and heavenly realms. The awesome (nôrāʾ) quality emphasizes that this boundary inspires reverent fear.
23: Under the expanse the wings of the living creatures stretched toward one another; each creature had two wings covering its body. The structural arrangement is now clarified: wings spread upward touching above the expanse; wings downward covering the body below. The upward-spreading wings that touch above the crystal expanse form the supporting structure for the divine throne; the body-covering wings maintain the creatures' modesty before the divine holiness seated above them.
24: The sound of their wings was like the roar of mighty waters (qol mayim rabim, qôl mayim rabbîm), like the voice of the Almighty (qol Shaddai, qôl Šadday), like the sound of an army camp. The triple simile for the sound: (1) the cosmic ocean (waters above; Genesis 1:7), (2) El Shaddai's own voice, and (3) a military host. Shaddai as the divine name connects to the patriarchal traditions (Genesis 17:1; Ezekiel 28:3); its use here alongside the more characteristic Ezekielian Adonai YHWH gives the sound an archaic patriarchal resonance. John's vision of the living creatures in Revelation 4:8 picks up the thunderous sound tradition.
25: When they stood still, they lowered their wings — the sound ceases when motion ceases. The dynamic responsiveness of the chariot (sound when moving, silence when stopped) mirrors the pattern of divine communication in Ezekiel: when the hand of YHWH is active, the prophet speaks; when the hand withdraws, the prophet is silent (Ezekiel 3:25-27). There was a voice above the expanse (qol mimal laragia) — the threshold between movement-noise and divine speech is the expanse that marks the creaturely-heavenly boundary.
26: even sapir (ʾeben sappîr): 'sapphire-like stone.' The lapis lazuli (the actual identification of biblical sappîr) forms the appearance of the throne. The sapphire throne appears in Moses's vision (Exodus 24:10: 'under his feet was something like a pavement made of lapis lazuli, clear as the sky') — Ezekiel's vision is a more elaborate and personal encounter with the same throne-reality. demut kisse (dĕmût kissēʾ): 'likeness of a throne' — Ezekiel uses demut (likeness) and mareh (appearance) throughout to maintain appropriate distance from unqualified assertion: he sees the likeness of the throne, not the throne itself. demut kemarat adam: 'likeness like the appearance of a human' — the enthroned form above the sapphire throne looks human. The anthropomorphic divine form will become central to Jewish mysticism (Shiur Qomah literature) and Christian incarnation theology.
27: hashmal (ḥašmal) again — from his loins upward, and again downward, surrounded by fire. The bipartite division of the enthroned figure — gleaming metal above, fire below — corresponds to fire both as judgment and as holiness. The brightness (nogah, nōgah) all around: the same word used in Ezekiel 1:4 for the brightness surrounding the initial theophanic cloud. The full circle of light that surrounds the enthroned figure is the divine kavod radiating outward — the glory both conceals and reveals the divine person.
